### FILE: environmental-issues-memo.docx
          ENVIRONMENTAL ISSUES MEMORANDUM  
          PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF PROPOSED TRANSACTION  
                          TO:  
          Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC — Acquisition Deal Team; Whitfield & Crane LLP (Buyer’s Environmental Counsel)  
                FROM:  
          Environmental Due Diligence Review  
                DATE:  
          October 28, 2024  
                RE:  
          Environmental Issues and Severity Ratings — Proposed Acquisition of 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611 (Seller: Breckenridge Manufacturing Co.)  
         
       1.0 Executive Summary  
      This memorandum summarizes the environmental issues identified during our review of the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (    “    Phase I ESA    ”    ) and supporting environmental and transactional documents prepared in connection with Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC’s (    “    Buyer    ”         or         “    Cornerstone    ”    ) proposed acquisition of the approximately 14.7-acre former manufacturing/warehouse complex located at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611 (the         “    Property    ”    ) from Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. (    “    Seller    ”    ). The acquisition is being financed in part by a $6,125,000 loan from Great Lakes Capital Bank (the         “    Lender    ”    ).  
          Overall Environmental Risk Rating: HIGH.         The Property was used for metal stamping, trichloroethylene (TCE) vapor degreasing (1961–1992), and hexavalent chromium/nickel electroplating (1965–2004) before being converted to warehouse use in December 2019. The Phase I ESA (Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc., dated October 25, 2024) identified two on-site recognized environmental conditions (    “    RECs    ”    ), and our review of the supporting record identifies several additional material concerns — including an off-site tetrachloroethylene (PCE) groundwater plume migrating toward the Property, an active leaking drum of waste TCE, a 39-year gap in hazardous waste disposal records, a prior Ohio EPA Notice of Violation, two unregistered underground storage tanks (    “    USTs    ”    ), and apparent inaccuracies in the Seller’s environmental representations and warranties.  
          Headline findings:  
              Two CRITICAL on-site RECs         — (i) former TCE vapor degreasing with elevated photoionization detector (    “    PID    ”    ) readings (42.3 ppm), no closure documentation, and a documented 1988 solvent spill; and (ii) 39 years of hexavalent chromium electroplating with visible chromium staining, an uncharacterized sump containing liquid with an iridescent sheen, and chemically degraded concrete.  
              A HIGH-severity off-site PCE groundwater plume         from the former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility (120 feet upgradient), with PCE at 87 µg/L (17.4× the Ohio standard of 5 µg/L) migrating south-southeast toward the Property, which lies within a designated Groundwater Management Zone. Notably, the Phase I ESA             did not classify this condition as a REC    , which appears inconsistent with the Lender’s express due-diligence requirements.  
              Apparent breaches of the Seller’s environmental representations         in the Asset Purchase Agreement (the         “    Purchase Agreement    ”    ), including the         “    no releases    ”         representation (§ 4.12(a)), the         “    no Environmental Notices    ”         representation (§ 4.12(c)), and the completeness of the UST schedule (§ 4.13 / Schedule 4.13).  
              Lender funding conditions are not satisfied.         Because RECs were identified, the Lender will not fund on the Phase I ESA alone; a Phase II ESA, Pollution Legal Liability insurance, and potentially a remediation escrow are required, on a timeline that runs concurrently with the Buyer’s October 31, 2024 termination right.  
          Headline recommendations:         (1) Commission a Phase II ESA immediately, scoped to both on-site RECs, the off-site PCE plume/vapor intrusion, the unregistered USTs, and the former outdoor drum storage area; (2) evaluate the § 6.3(b) termination right before October 31, 2024; (3) demand Seller corrections to the environmental representations and Schedule 4.13, and negotiate price/indemnity/escrow concessions; (4) satisfy the Lender’s Phase II/insurance/escrow deliverables by the November 15–30, 2024 deadlines; and (5) arrange immediate removal of the abandoned waste TCE drum.             Closing should not occur until the Phase II results, Lender conditions, and representation issues are resolved.  
      
        2.0 Scope of Review and Documents Examined  
      The following documents were reviewed in preparing this memorandum:  
              Phase I ESA         — Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc.,             Phase I Environmental Site Assessment, 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio    , Project No. GEC-2024-0347, dated October 25, 2024 (site reconnaissance October 2, 2024; ASTM E1527-21).  
              EDR Database Summary         — Environmental Data Resources, Inc., Radius Map Report summary pages, dated September 15, 2024 (subject property, adjacent property, and orphan site listings).  
              Ohio EPA Response         — Ohio EPA, Division of Environmental Response and Revitalization, Northwest District Office, records response dated September 28, 2024 (File Ref. DERR-NW-2024-RR-0438).  
              Lender Requirements Letter         — Great Lakes Capital Bank, environmental due diligence requirements letter dated September 10, 2024 (Loan No. GLCB-2024-08173).  
              Purchase Agreement Excerpt         — Asset Purchase Agreement between Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. and Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC, dated September 1, 2024 (Article I definitions; §§ 4.12–4.13; § 6.3; §§ 8.1–8.4; Schedule 4.13).  
          Scope note — documents pertaining to a separate property.         Three additional documents were provided that pertain to a             different property and a different seller    : a Phase I ESA for 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio (Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc., dated April 18, 2025; seller Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc.), a 2004 UST Closure Report for that Dayton property (Redland Environmental Services), and a GeoSearch environmental database summary for that Dayton property. These documents relate to a separate, later transaction and are             not part of the subject acquisition    . They have been reviewed for completeness but are outside the scope of this memorandum and are not relied upon in the findings below. (We note, for avoidance of doubt, that the Dayton Phase I ESA was prepared under the older ASTM E1527-13 standard, whereas the subject acquisition and the Lender require ASTM E1527-21.)  
      
        3.0 Severity Rating Definitions  
      Each issue is assigned a severity rating using the following scale, which reflects the potential risk to human health and the environment, the magnitude of potential remediation cost and legal liability, and the potential effect on transaction viability and Lender funding.  
                         Rating  
          Definition  
                CRITICAL  
          Conditions involving known or strongly indicated releases of hazardous substances at the Property (including carcinogenic solvents or heavy metals), active/ongoing releases, or fundamental deficiencies that may invalidate due diligence or Lender acceptance. Likely to require subsurface investigation and remediation, and may jeopardize the transaction or Lender funding.  
                HIGH  
          Significant environmental liabilities, substantial remediation-cost exposure, material legal/transactional risk, or conditions the Lender expressly requires to be treated as RECs. Require Phase II investigation, remediation, insurance/escrow, or contract remediation before closing.  
                MEDIUM  
          Conditions warranting further investigation, monitoring, or contractual protection, or notable data gaps/classification concerns that are not, by themselves, immediately deal-threatening.  
                LOW  
          Routine regulatory statuses, de minimis conditions, or informational items requiring awareness or follow-up but no immediate action.  
         
        4.0 Issues Summary  
                           No.  
          Issue  
          Source / Classification  
          Severity  
            1  
          Former TCE vapor degreasing — potential chlorinated solvent (DNAPL) release to soil/groundwater  
          Phase I ESA REC No. 1  
              CRITICAL  
            2  
          Former hexavalent chromium electroplating — potential metals release; uncharacterized sump liquid  
          Phase I ESA REC No. 2  
              CRITICAL  
            3  
          Abandoned, leaking 55-gallon drum of waste TCE  
          Phase I ESA REC No. 1 (observed)  
              HIGH  
            4  
          Off-site PCE groundwater plume (Lake Erie Dry Cleaning) migrating toward the Property  
          EDR / Ohio EPA response  
              HIGH  
            5  
          Phase I ESA did not classify the off-site PCE plume as a REC — inconsistent with Lender requirements  
          Phase I ESA vs. Lender letter  
              HIGH  
            6  
          Unregistered/uncharacterized historical USTs (2,000-gal waste solvent; 500-gal heating oil)  
          Sanborn maps / Ohio EPA / EDR  
              HIGH  
            7  
          39-year gap in hazardous waste disposal records (1965–2003)  
          Ohio EPA response / Phase I ESA  
              HIGH  
            8  
          2003 Ohio EPA NOV and consent order — leaking chromium drums; resolution unverified  
          Ohio EPA response  
              HIGH  
            9  
          Former outdoor drum storage area — stressed vegetation; historical hazardous waste storage  
          Phase I ESA / aerial photos / Ohio EPA  
              HIGH  
            10  
          Combined vapor intrusion risk (on-site TCE + off-site PCE)  
          Phase I ESA / Ohio EPA / Lender letter  
              HIGH  
            11  
          Seller’s environmental representations appear inaccurate/incomplete (breach risk)  
          Purchase Agreement vs. record  
              HIGH  
            12  
          Lender funding conditions not satisfied — Phase II/insurance/escrow triggered  
          Lender letter / Phase I ESA  
              HIGH  
            13  
          Purchase Agreement termination right — $500,000 REC remediation threshold; Oct. 31 deadline  
          Purchase Agreement § 6.3(b)  
              HIGH  
            14  
          Indemnification cap/basket/survival may be insufficient  
          Purchase Agreement §§ 8.2, 8.4  
              MEDIUM  
            15  
          Seller non-cooperation and statements inconsistent with documented facts  
          Phase I ESA interviews  
              MEDIUM  
            16  
          Uncharacterized monitoring well         “    MW-1    ”         — unknown origin/purpose  
          Phase I ESA / EDR  
              MEDIUM  
            17  
          Data gaps affecting AAI completeness and Lender         “    unacceptable risk    ”         review  
          Phase I ESA / Ohio EPA  
              MEDIUM  
            18  
          Non-scope considerations — asbestos-containing materials and lead-based paint  
          Phase I ESA (non-scope)  
              LOW  
            19  
          Active 10,000-gal diesel UST (registered/compliant) and RCRA SQG status  
          EDR / Ohio EPA  
              LOW  
            20  
          Other adjacent/nearby listings (closed LUSTs, compliant facilities)  
          EDR  
              LOW  
         
        5.0 Detailed Discussion of Issues  
       5.1 On-Site Recognized Environmental Conditions  
       Issue 1 — Former TCE Vapor Degreasing: Potential Chlorinated Solvent Release to Soil and Groundwater  
          Severity: CRITICAL         |             Phase I ESA REC No. 1  
      TCE was used as a vapor degreasing solvent in the northwest corner of the main manufacturing building from 1961 through approximately 1992 — a period of more than 30 years. The Phase I ESA documented the following indicators of a release to the subsurface:  
              Elevated PID reading of 42.3 ppm         recorded at a crack in the floor slab of the former degreasing area, against background readings of less than 0.5 ppm elsewhere in the building — a strong indicator of residual volatile organic compound vapors emanating from beneath the slab.  
              Visible dark staining         penetrating the concrete floor slab, with multiple cracks (up to ¼-inch), and staining on interior walls up to ~3 feet (consistent with vapor condensation/splash).  
              No closure documentation, decommissioning records, or confirmation sampling         was located for the degreasing area following the reported cessation of TCE use in 1992.  
          A             1988 chemical spill         of         “    unknown quantity of degreasing solvent    ”         from an overturned drum is documented in City of Toledo Fire Department records; cleanup was performed by Seller’s employees with no follow-up environmental assessment.  
          Why CRITICAL.         TCE is a known human carcinogen and a dense, persistent chlorinated solvent that forms dense non-aqueous phase liquids (DNAPL) capable of penetrating deep into aquifers and generating long-lived groundwater plumes and soil-vapor sources. The combination of 30+ years of use, an elevated PID reading two orders of magnitude above background, undocumented closure, and a documented spill presents a strong likelihood of a release to soil and potentially groundwater beneath the building. This condition drives both direct-contact/vapor-intrusion risk and potentially significant remediation cost. A Phase II ESA (soil borings through the slab, groundwater monitoring wells, and soil-vapor sampling) is required.  
        Issue 2 — Former Hexavalent Chromium Electroplating: Potential Metals Release; Uncharacterized Sump Liquid  
          Severity: CRITICAL         |             Phase I ESA REC No. 2  
      Electroplating using hexavalent chromium and nickel solutions was conducted in the south wing of the main building from 1965 through 2004 — approximately 39 years. Observed indicators include:  
              Prominent blue-green staining         on concrete floors and lower walls (to ~4 feet), consistent with chromium residues, concentrated where plating tanks were historically located.  
              Chemical degradation (pitting, etching, surface erosion)         of the concrete floor consistent with prolonged exposure to acidic/alkaline plating solutions — a pathway for migration through the slab.  
          An             in-ground concrete sump         (~3 ft × 3 ft × ~3 ft deep) in the center of the room containing approximately             four inches of liquid with a visible iridescent sheen         and a greenish tint; the source and composition are undetermined (not sampled).  
          Hazardous waste disposal documentation exists             only for 2004         (six manifests; 14,200 lbs; RCRA codes D007 chromium and F006 electroplating sludge).             No manifests or disposal records exist for 1965–2003         (see Issue 7).  
          A             2003 Ohio EPA Notice of Violation         for improper hazardous waste storage (see Issue 8).  
          Why CRITICAL.         Hexavalent chromium is highly toxic and carcinogenic. Thirty-nine years of plating operations with visible residual contamination, an uncharacterized sump containing liquid with a sheen, and a chemically degraded concrete floor present a strong likelihood of metals release to the subsurface. The sump liquid itself may constitute an uncharacterized hazardous waste accumulation. A Phase II ESA is required, including characterization of the sump liquid, soil borings through/beneath the slab, and groundwater monitoring wells analyzed for hexavalent and total chromium, nickel, and related metals.  
         5.2 Active / Hazardous Conditions  
       Issue 3 — Abandoned, Leaking 55-Gallon Drum of Waste TCE  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Phase I ESA REC No. 1 (observed)  
      A 55-gallon drum labeled         “    WASTE TRICHLOROETHYLENE    ”         was observed on the ground surface behind the maintenance/utility building. The drum is approximately half full, with a             rusted bottom and evidence of past leakage    . Stained soil (~3 ft × 3 ft, dark/oily, vegetation absent) is present beneath the drum. The Seller stated the drum has been there         “    for years,    ”         “    probably from the early ’90s.    ”  
          Why HIGH.         This is an             active, ongoing release         of a listed hazardous waste (TCE) to soil at the Property — a condition that is both an environmental liability and a RCRA compliance concern (improper storage/accumulation of hazardous waste). Although the areal extent appears limited and the drum is readily removable, an active leaking drum of a carcinogenic solvent warrants             immediate removal and disposal by a licensed transporter    , followed by characterization of the stained soil. This should not await the Phase II.  
         5.3 Off-Site Contamination  
       Issue 4 — Off-Site PCE Groundwater Plume (Lake Erie Dry Cleaning) Migrating Toward the Property  
          Severity: HIGH         |             EDR Database Summary; Ohio EPA response  
      The former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive is located approximately             120 feet north         of the Property and is             hydraulically upgradient    . Key facts, confirmed by both the EDR report and the Ohio EPA response:  
          A confirmed             PCE groundwater contamination plume         is documented (Ohio EPA Master Sites List; VAP ID NW-0923).  
          Monitoring well             MW-7         (located ~120 feet north of the Property boundary) detected             PCE at 87 µg/L         —             17.4× the Ohio generic groundwater standard of 5 µg/L    .  
          The plume migrates             south-southeast — toward the Property         — consistent with regional groundwater flow.  
          The Property is located within a             designated Groundwater Management Zone (        “        GMZ        ”        )         established by Ohio EPA for this plume.  
          A             Covenant Not to Sue         was issued under the Ohio VAP in 2018, but it             applies exclusively to the dry cleaner parcel and does not address off-site migration         onto the Property.  
          An environmental covenant recorded against the dry cleaner parcel restricts groundwater use and requires maintenance of a sub-slab vapor mitigation system there.  
              No investigation of PCE in groundwater beneath the Property has been conducted    , and the lateral/vertical extent of plume migration onto the Property is             undelineated    .  
          Why HIGH.         A documented carcinogenic-solvent plume at 17× the standard is migrating toward the Property from 120 feet away, the Property lies within the associated GMZ, and no on-site investigation has confirmed or ruled out impacts. This presents both groundwater contamination and vapor intrusion risk (see Issue 10). Depending on Phase II results, this could escalate to CRITICAL. The GMZ designation may also restrict future groundwater withdrawals/land use.  
        Issue 5 — Phase I ESA Did Not Classify the Off-Site PCE Plume as a REC  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Phase I ESA vs. Lender Requirements Letter  
      The Phase I ESA describes the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning PCE plume in detail in its records-review section (§ 5.1.1) and EDR appendix, but             does not list it as a REC, CREC, HREC, or de minimis condition in its Findings and Conclusions (§ 9.0)    . The report’s findings identify only REC No. 1 (TCE) and REC No. 2 (electroplating).  
      This appears inconsistent with the Lender’s express requirements. The Lender’s letter (§ 2) states that the Phase I ESA         “    must evaluate off-site sources of contamination that may be migrating onto or otherwise affecting the subject Property, including … contaminated groundwater plumes from adjacent or nearby properties,    ”         and that         “    any such conditions             must be identified as recognized environmental conditions    .    ”         The Lender specifically referenced the former dry cleaning facility at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive.  
          Why HIGH.         The omission creates two risks: (i) a potential             AAI-compliance gap         — the Lender’s environmental counsel (Whitfield & Crane LLP) will independently review the Phase I ESA for AAI compliance and may reject or require supplementation of the report; and (ii) a             misalignment between the due-diligence deliverable and the Lender’s funding conditions    , which could delay or jeopardize loan funding. We recommend that the environmental consultant be directed to supplement the report to expressly classify the off-site PCE plume (and the GMZ/vapor-intrusion implications) as a REC, or to provide a written rationale if it declines to do so. Under ASTM E1527-21, the documented migration of a hazardous-substance plume toward a property for which on-site impacts have not been ruled out is, at minimum, a condition warranting REC classification and Phase II evaluation.  
         5.4 Historical Underground Storage Tanks  
       Issue 6 — Unregistered/Uncharacterized Historical USTs (2,000-Gallon Waste Solvent; 500-Gallon Heating Oil)  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Sanborn maps; Ohio EPA response; EDR  
      Two historical USTs appear on Sanborn fire insurance maps but have             no registration, closure, removal, or site-assessment records         in the Ohio BUSTR database (confirmed by Ohio EPA):  
              2,000-gallon waste solvent UST         — depicted on the 1987 Sanborn map on the east side of the Property, between the main building and the eastern boundary, in the vicinity of the former outdoor drum storage area (see Issue 9).         “    Waste solvent    ”         is consistent with the chlorinated solvents historically used at the facility. Its current status is             unknown — it may remain in place    .  
              500-gallon heating oil UST         — depicted on the 1948 and 1955 Sanborn maps serving the Halstead family farmhouse (northwest corner), which was demolished in 1961. The Phase I ESA classified this as an HREC on the assumption it was removed with the farmhouse, but             no removal documentation exists         and the assumption is unverified.  
          Why HIGH.         Two unregistered USTs with no closure documentation represent potential unidentified subsurface sources, particularly the 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST given its contents and proximity to the former drum storage area. Ohio EPA expressly recommended geophysical surveys, interviews, and review of fire department permit records to determine the status of these tanks. The Phase I ESA’s classification of the heating oil UST as an HREC rests on an unverified assumption and should be reconsidered pending a geophysical survey. This is also a Seller-representation issue (see Issue 11): Schedule 4.13 lists only the active 10,000-gallon diesel UST and states that         “    No other Underground Storage Tanks are or have been located on the Property.    ”  
         5.5 Hazardous Waste Management and Regulatory History  
       Issue 7 — 39-Year Gap in Hazardous Waste Disposal Records (1965–2003)  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Ohio EPA response; Phase I ESA  
      Ohio EPA confirmed that the only hazardous waste manifests/biennial reports on file for the facility are for reporting year 2004 (six manifests; 14,200 lbs; D007 and F006).             No manifests or generation reports exist for 1965 through 2003         — the entire 39-year period of electroplating operations. The Seller stated that pre-2004 records were         “    not available    ”         /         “    not retained.    ”  
          Why HIGH.         The near-total absence of disposal documentation for four decades of chromium- and solvent-bearing waste generation is a significant data gap that raises questions about historical waste management practices and creates potential RCRA/CERCLA exposure (including for off-site disposal sites at which Buyer, as the current owner, could face successor liability or contribution claims). This gap also undermines the reliability of the Seller’s compliance representations (§ 4.12(b)) and document-production representation (§ 4.12(d)).  
        Issue 8 — 2003 Ohio EPA Notice of Violation and Consent Order; Resolution Unverified  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Ohio EPA response  
      A July 17, 2003 Ohio EPA Compliance Evaluation Inspection documented: (i)             improper storage of hazardous waste in rusted, visibly leaking 55-gallon drums         in an outdoor area on the east side, containing electroplating sludge and spent chromium-bearing wastewater, with staining beneath the drums; (ii) failure to label containers with accumulation start dates; and (iii) failure to maintain hazardous waste determination records. Ohio EPA issued a             Notice of Violation (File No. NW-2003-0471)         in August 2003, resolved through a             Director’s Final Findings and Orders (consent order)         requiring a facility-wide waste audit, proper characterization/disposal of accumulated waste, a written waste management plan, and a compliance certification within 120 days.  
          Critical caveat:         The consent order file (including the signed order and any compliance certification) is             archived at Ohio EPA’s central repository and was not produced    ; Ohio EPA states only that the matter was         “    reportedly resolved.    ”         The Seller could not produce a copy of the consent order.  
          Why HIGH.         This is a documented regulatory violation involving leaking drums of chromium waste — directly relevant to the electroplating REC (Issue 2) and the former drum storage area (Issue 9). The inability to verify completion of the consent order’s corrective actions is a material data gap. This NOV is also an         “    Environmental Notice    ”         that does not appear on Schedule 4.12(c) (which states         “    None    ”    ), implicating the Seller’s representations (Issue 11). Buyer should obtain the consent order and compliance certification from Ohio EPA Central Office (30–45 business days) before closing.  
        Issue 9 — Former Outdoor Drum Storage Area with Stressed Vegetation  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Phase I ESA; aerial photographs; Ohio EPA  
      An approximately 40 ft × 60 ft area on the east side of the Property, delineated by deteriorating concrete curbing, exhibits             stressed/sparse, yellowed vegetation         relative to surrounding areas. Historical aerial photographs (1990, 2000) show an estimated 100+ drums stored on this unpaved surface. The 1987 Sanborn map places the 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST in this vicinity (Issue 6). The 2003 Ohio EPA inspection found rusted, leaking drums of electroplating sludge/chromium waste in an outdoor east-side storage area — likely this location. The Seller acknowledged,         “    We used to keep drums out there before the EPA came around,    ”         but could not detail the materials stored.  
          Why HIGH.         Historical outdoor storage of hazardous waste (chromium sludge and solvents) on unpaved ground without secondary containment, with confirmed leaking drums in 2003 and current stressed vegetation, indicates a potential soil (and possibly groundwater) release. The Phase I ESA noted this area in its reconnaissance (§ 6.2.4) but did not classify it as a separate REC in its findings. The Phase II should include soil borings in this area.  
         5.6 Vapor Intrusion  
       Issue 10 — Combined Vapor Intrusion Risk (On-Site TCE + Off-Site PCE)  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Phase I ESA; Ohio EPA; Lender letter  
      The Property is subject to vapor intrusion risk from two volatile chlorinated-solvent sources: (i) on-site TCE in the subsurface beneath the former degreasing area (elevated PID; Issue 1) and (ii) the off-site PCE plume migrating toward the Property (Issue 4). The Property is currently used for warehousing (occupied structures). The Lender’s requirements (§ 3.B.5) mandate a             vapor intrusion assessment         if VOCs are identified in soil or groundwater at or beneath the Property, and a             vapor mitigation plan         for any occupied/occupiable structure if risks warrant. The adjacent dry cleaner parcel is itself subject to a vapor mitigation requirement under its environmental covenant.  
          Why HIGH.         Vapor intrusion poses potential human-health risk to warehouse occupants and is a Lender condition precedent. The Phase II should include sub-slab soil-vapor and indoor-air sampling, and the acquisition plan should budget for potential vapor mitigation systems.  
         5.7 Transactional, Legal, and Lender Issues  
       Issue 11 — Seller’s Environmental Representations Appear Inaccurate/Incomplete (Breach Risk)  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Purchase Agreement §§ 4.12–4.13 vs. documented record  
      Comparison of the Seller’s representations against the documented record reveals apparent inaccuracies:  
              § 4.12(a) (        “        no Release        ”        ) — Schedule 4.12(a):                 “        None.        ”         Documented releases include the 1988 degreasing-solvent spill (Toledo Fire Department), the active leaking waste TCE drum (Issue 3), and the 2003 leaking chromium-waste drums (Issue 8). The         “    to Seller’s knowledge    ”         qualifier is strained as to the 2003 release, which Ohio EPA documented at the Seller’s facility during the tenure of Gerald Breckenridge III (involved since the mid-1980s).  
              § 4.12(b) (material compliance with Environmental Laws).         The 2003 NOV for improper hazardous waste storage is inconsistent with         “    at all times … material compliance.    ”  
              § 4.12(c) (        “        no Environmental Notice        ”        ) — Schedule 4.12(c):                 “        None.        ”         The 2003 NOV is an         “    Environmental Notice    ”         as defined in the Purchase Agreement. The Seller acknowledged the NOV to the consultant but did not disclose it on the schedule.  
              § 4.12(d) (delivery of all material environmental documents).         The consent order, 39 years of waste disposal records, corporate records (1961–1990), and any records associated with monitoring well MW-1 were not produced.  
              § 4.13 / Schedule 4.13 (complete UST list).         The schedule lists only the active 10,000-gallon diesel UST and states         “    No other Underground Storage Tanks are or have been located on the Property,    ”         omitting the 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST and 500-gallon heating oil UST (Issue 6).  
          Why HIGH.         These are potential breaches of the Seller’s representations and warranties, giving rise to indemnification claims under § 8.2(a)(i) and (a)(iii) (Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities). Importantly, the Seller’s statements to the consultant — denying knowledge of the 1988 spill (    “    not aware of any spills    ”    ), claiming         “    all the chemicals were handled properly,    ”         and characterizing the NOV as         “    resolved years ago    ”         — are inconsistent with documented facts and may support an argument of             fraud or intentional misrepresentation    , which under § 8.4(d) is             not subject to the Basket, the Indemnification Cap, or the Survival Period    . Buyer should demand that the Seller correct the representations and Schedule 4.13 before closing and should preserve all evidence of the inconsistencies.  
        Issue 12 — Lender Funding Conditions Not Satisfied; Phase II/Insurance/Escrow Triggered  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Lender letter § 3.B; Phase I ESA  
      Because the Phase I ESA identified RECs, the Lender             will not fund the Loan on the Phase I ESA alone    . The following become conditions precedent to funding:  
              Phase II ESA         (ASTM E1903-19) with quantified remediation cost estimates (best-case, most-likely, worst-case), including methodology, standards, and time to completion.  
              Pollution Legal Liability (PLL) insurance         — limits of the greater of the worst-case estimate or $5,000,000, 10-year term, carrier rated A− or better, with the Bank as additional insured and loss payee. (Non-negotiable where RECs are identified.)  
              Environmental Escrow         — if the most-likely estimate exceeds $250,000, an escrow of 125% of the most-likely estimate, drawable only with the Bank’s approval.  
              Remediation Plan         — if estimated costs exceed $500,000 (most-likely or worst-case), a plan demonstrating remediation within 36 months, approved by a licensed professional and (where applicable) Ohio EPA.  
              Vapor Intrusion Assessment         — required because VOCs are indicated (Issue 10).  
          Delivery deadlines:         Phase I ESA by             November 15, 2024    ; Phase II ESA, PLL binder, and escrow evidence by             November 30, 2024         (closing December 15, 2024). The Lender may decline to fund if worst-case remediation exceeds 50% of appraised value, if the Property is listed/proposed on the NPL, if subject to an active RCRA corrective-action order, or if unresolved data gaps prevent adequate risk characterization (§ 3.C).  
          Why HIGH.         The RECs trigger a cascade of time-sensitive, cost-bearing conditions. The timeline is compressed (Phase II by November 30), and the cost estimates will drive the insurance/escrow/remediation-plan requirements and the Lender’s         “    unacceptable risk    ”         determination. Buyer should commission the Phase II immediately and engage an insurance broker for the PLL policy in parallel.  
        Issue 13 — Purchase Agreement Termination Right; $500,000 REC Remediation Threshold  
          Severity: HIGH         |             Purchase Agreement § 6.3(b)  
      Under § 6.3(b), Buyer may terminate the Purchase Agreement and recover its $437,500 earnest money if the Phase I ESA (or any environmental investigation during the due-diligence period) identifies RECs that, in Buyer’s reasonable judgment, could result in             Remediation Costs exceeding $500,000    , by written notice delivered             no later than 5:00 p.m. ET on October 31, 2024    . If Buyer does not terminate by that date, the right is             deemed waived         (§ 6.3(c)), though indemnification rights survive.  
          Why HIGH.         Given the nature and extent of the identified conditions — TCE DNAPL, 39 years of hexavalent chromium plating, an off-site PCE plume, unregistered USTs, and a former drum storage area — remediation costs could readily exceed $500,000. The Phase I ESA is dated October 25, 2024, leaving only             six days         to evaluate and exercise the termination right. Buyer should promptly obtain a preliminary remediation-cost scoping opinion from its environmental consultant to inform the termination decision, and should consider whether to (a) exercise the termination right, (b) negotiate a price reduction/indemnity enhancement/escrow in lieu of termination, or (c) proceed subject to the Phase II and Lender conditions. Note that the § 6.3(b) termination right does not extend to post-due-diligence investigations (§ 6.3(d)).  
        Issue 14 — Indemnification Cap, Basket, and Survival Period May Be Insufficient  
          Severity: MEDIUM         |             Purchase Agreement §§ 8.2, 8.4  
      The Seller’s indemnification for Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities (§ 8.2(a)(iii)) is subject to: a             Basket of $87,500         (1% of price; a true deductible that does             not         apply to environmental claims under § 8.2(a)(iii)); an             Indemnification Cap of $1,750,000         (20% of price); and a             Survival Period of three (3) years    . The Cap and Survival Period apply to environmental claims.  
          Why MEDIUM.         Remediation of chlorinated-solvent and hexavalent chromium contamination, plus potential off-site PCE response costs, could exceed the $1,750,000 Cap, leaving Buyer uninsured against the excess. A three-year survival period is relatively short for latent contamination that may be discovered later. Mitigants: (i) the             fraud/intentional misrepresentation exception         (§ 8.4(d)) bypasses the Cap, Basket, and Survival Period — potentially significant given Issue 11; (ii) the Lender-required PLL insurance (Issue 12) provides a separate backstop; and (iii) Buyer may negotiate an increased Cap, extended survival, or a dedicated environmental escrow. Buyer should not rely on Seller indemnity alone.  
        Issue 15 — Seller Non-Cooperation and Statements Inconsistent with Documented Facts  
          Severity: MEDIUM         |             Phase I ESA interviews  
      The Seller (Gerald Breckenridge III) declined to produce corporate records (1961–1990) (    “    in storage    ”    ), could not produce 39 years of waste disposal records, denied knowledge of the 1988 spill despite the Fire Department record (    “    not aware of any spills    ”    ;         “    a minor incident … nothing to worry about    ”    ), claimed         “    all the chemicals were handled properly    ”         despite the 2003 NOV, and disclaimed knowledge of monitoring well MW-1 and the former drum storage area.  
          Why MEDIUM.         The Seller’s lack of cooperation and inconsistent statements undermine the reliability of seller-provided information, create AAI data gaps (Issue 17), and support heightened scrutiny of the representations (Issue 11). The inconsistencies are also relevant to the fraud/intentional-misrepresentation analysis under § 8.4(d). Buyer should document these inconsistencies and consider requesting specific document production as a condition to closing.  
         5.8 Data Gaps and Investigation  
       Issue 16 — Uncharacterized Monitoring Well         “    MW-1    ”  
          Severity: MEDIUM         |             Phase I ESA; EDR  
      A monitoring well labeled         “    MW-1    ”         was observed near the northeast property boundary, with a damaged surface cover and accumulated debris. No installation logs, construction details, or analytical data were located, and the Seller disclaimed knowledge of its origin or purpose.  
          Why MEDIUM.         The presence of an uncharacterized monitoring well suggests a prior environmental investigation that is undocumented and undisclosed — a potential indicator of known contamination (possibly related to the off-site PCE plume, given the well’s northeast location near the upgradient dry cleaner). Buyer should research well installation records (Ohio Department of Natural Resources; Ohio EPA) and consider sampling MW-1 as part of the Phase II. The damaged well is also a physical/safety and potential conduit concern.  
        Issue 17 — Data Gaps Affecting AAI Completeness and Lender         “    Unacceptable Risk    ”         Review  
          Severity: MEDIUM         |             Phase I ESA; Ohio EPA  
      Material data gaps include: the 39-year hazardous waste disposal record gap (Issue 7); unproduced corporate records (1961–1990); the unproduced/archived 2003 consent order and compliance certification (Issue 8); the absence of closure records for two unregistered USTs (Issue 6); and the absence of any records for MW-1 (Issue 16). The Lender will         “    independently assess the materiality of any unresolved data gaps    ”         and may decline funding if unresolved gaps         “    prevent adequate characterization of the environmental risk    ”         (Lender letter § 3.C(d)).  
          Why MEDIUM.         While the Phase I ESA concluded the data gaps are not expected to significantly affect findings, the Lender retains discretion to deem them material. Several gaps (waste disposal records, consent order) are directly relevant to the RECs and to the Seller’s representations. Buyer should pursue the outstanding records (notably the consent order from Ohio EPA Central Office) and supplement the due diligence accordingly.  
         5.9 Non-Scope and Informational Items  
       Issue 18 — Non-Scope Considerations: Asbestos-Containing Materials and Lead-Based Paint  
          Severity: LOW         |             Phase I ESA (non-scope)  
      The main building (1962/1978), maintenance building (1972), and warehouse (1985) predate the 1980s ACM phase-out and the 1978 LBP ban. No ACM or lead-based paint surveys were performed (non-scope under ASTM E1527-21). If renovation, demolition, or disturbance of suspect materials is anticipated (e.g., for redevelopment), Buyer should commission ACM and LBP surveys and budget for abatement and TSCA/NESHAP compliance.  
        Issue 19 — Active 10,000-Gallon Diesel UST and RCRA SQG Status  
          Severity: LOW         |             EDR; Ohio EPA  
              10,000-gallon diesel UST         (Ohio Facility ID 48-22-10-0087): active, registered, installed 1978, cathodic-protection upgrade 1998, last inspected May 2022 with no violations; no evidence of release. Note: the Lender’s post-closing covenants (Lender letter § 6(d)) prohibit installing, using, operating, or closing any UST without the Bank’s prior written consent — Buyer should confirm continued-use intent and Lender consent.  
              RCRA SQG status         (EPA ID OHD044891237): consistent with historical operations; no current violations in ECHO. Because manufacturing ceased in December 2019, Buyer should verify the current generator status and confirm no hazardous waste remains on-site.  
        Issue 20 — Other Adjacent/Nearby Listings  
          Severity: LOW         |             EDR  
      Other nearby listings are closed or compliant and are not expected to materially affect the Property: Toledo Coatings & Adhesives Inc. (east; CERCLIS NFA, 1999); Bayshore Auto Salvage (NE; closed LUST, plume migrated east/away); Former Sunoco Station #4477 (SSW; closed LUST, downgradient); Metro Petroleum (NNE; modern compliant facility); Great Lakes Metal Finishing Inc. (ENE; compliant SQG, no releases); and the Norfolk Southern Railroad right-of-way (west; de minimis herbicide application). The Lucas County sanitary sewer trunk line (south) is noted as a potential preferential pathway but has no associated contamination.  
      
          6.0 Cross-Cutting Transactional Analysis  
          Lender requirements mapping.         The identified RECs (Issues 1, 2) and the off-site PCE plume (Issue 4) trigger the full § 3.B conditional regime: Phase II with cost estimates, PLL insurance (≥ $5M / 10 years), potential escrow (if most-likely > $250,000), potential remediation plan (if > $500,000), and a vapor intrusion assessment (Issue 10). The Phase II cost estimates will determine whether the Lender’s         “    unacceptable risk    ”         thresholds (§ 3.C) are implicated — particularly the 50%-of-appraised-value worst-case ceiling. The Phase I ESA’s failure to classify the PCE plume as a REC (Issue 5) should be cured before the Lender’s review to avoid rejection or delay.  
          Purchase Agreement mapping.         The § 6.3(b) termination right (Issue 13) and the October 31, 2024 deadline are the most time-critical decision points. The Seller’s apparent representation breaches (Issue 11) provide leverage for renegotiation (price, indemnity, escrow, or seller-funded Phase II/remediation) and a potential fraud-exception path outside the indemnity Cap (Issue 14). The indemnity Cap ($1.75M) and three-year survival may be inadequate relative to potential remediation costs; the Lender-required PLL insurance is the primary backstop.  
          Timeline.         The sequence is compressed: Phase I ESA complete (Oct. 25) → termination decision (Oct. 31) → Phase I to Lender (Nov. 15) → Phase II + PLL + escrow to Lender (Nov. 30) → closing (Dec. 15). The Phase II and PLL procurement must begin immediately to meet the November 30 deadline. The 30–45 business-day retrieval time for the archived consent order (Issue 8) may extend beyond closing; Buyer should request it now and consider making closing conditional on its review.  
      
        7.0 Recommendations and Next Steps  
              Commission a Phase II ESA immediately    , scoped to: (a) the former TCE degreasing area (soil borings through the slab, groundwater wells, soil-vapor sampling); (b) the former electroplating room (sump liquid characterization, soil borings, groundwater wells for Cr(VI)/total Cr/nickel); (c) the abandoned TCE drum location (soil borings); (d) the former outdoor drum storage area and the 2,000-gal waste solvent UST vicinity (soil borings, geophysical survey for the UST); (e) the off-site PCE plume (groundwater wells on the Property, sub-slab/indoor-air vapor sampling); and (f) sampling of monitoring well MW-1. Obtain best-/most-likely/worst-case remediation cost estimates.  
              Remove the abandoned waste TCE drum immediately         (Issue 3) under proper hazardous waste protocols, with characterization of the stained soil.  
              Evaluate the § 6.3(b) termination right before October 31, 2024         using a preliminary remediation-cost scoping opinion; decide among termination, renegotiation, or proceeding subject to conditions.  
              Direct the environmental consultant to supplement the Phase I ESA         to classify the off-site PCE plume/GMZ/vapor-intrusion condition as a REC (Issue 5), curing the apparent inconsistency with the Lender’s requirements.  
              Demand Seller corrections         to the environmental representations (§§ 4.12(a), (b), (c), (d)) and Schedule 4.13, and negotiate concessions: price reduction, increased Indemnification Cap/extended survival, seller-funded Phase II or remediation, and/or a dedicated environmental escrow. Preserve evidence of the Seller’s inconsistent statements for the fraud/intentional-misrepresentation analysis (§ 8.4(d)).  
              Engage an insurance broker         to procure the Lender-required PLL policy (≥ $5M / 10 years / A− or better; Bank as additional insured and loss payee) in parallel with the Phase II.  
              Request the archived 2003 consent order and compliance certification         from Ohio EPA Central Office immediately; consider making closing conditional on review.  
              Research monitoring well MW-1         (ODNR/Ohio EPA records) and the two unregistered USTs (geophysical survey; fire department permit records).  
              Do not close         until the Phase II results, Lender conditions (Phase II, PLL, escrow, remediation plan, vapor assessment), and the Seller-representation issues are resolved.  
      
        8.0 Limitations  
      This memorandum is based solely on the documents listed in Section 2.0 and was prepared to assist Buyer and its counsel in evaluating environmental issues in connection with the proposed acquisition. It is not a Phase I or Phase II Environmental Site Assessment, does not constitute a legal opinion, and does not independently verify the accuracy of information provided by third parties, the Seller, or governmental agencies. Severity ratings reflect professional judgment as of the date of this memorandum based on the information reviewed and are subject to revision based on the results of the recommended Phase II ESA and any additional information obtained. This memorandum is privileged and confidential, prepared in anticipation of the proposed transaction, and intended for the use of Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC and its counsel.

### FILE: credential-gap-analysis.docx
          MEMORANDUM  
          PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF AUDIT OR LITIGATION  
                          TO:  
          Craig Halloran, Esq.; Case File (A-18247-63910)  
                FROM:  
          Immigration Practice Group, Whitmore & Callahan LLP  
                DATE:  
          June 5, 2025  
                RE:  
          Credential Gap Analysis — PERM Application (ETA-9089), Case No. A-18247-63910 —             Prismex Analytics, Inc.         (employer/petitioner) v.             Dr. Ananya Mehrotra         (beneficiary)  
                POSITION:  
          Senior Machine Learning Engineer (SOC 15-2051, Data Scientists, Wage Level III)  
         
       I. Purpose and Scope  
      This memorandum reviews the certified Application for Permanent Employment Certification (ETA Form 9089, PERM Case No. A-18247-63910, filed March 15, 2025; certified May 22, 2025) against the beneficiary’s supporting credentials to identify any gaps between (a) the minimum requirements and attestations stated on the application and (b) the education, experience, and certifications actually documented in the file. For each gap, we provide a risk assessment and remedial recommendation. This review is undertaken in connection with the employer’s June 4, 2025 request that counsel confirm         “    whether there are any issues with the credentials matching the PERM requirements    ”         and identify any missing or supplemental documentation.  
          Important context.         The PERM has been certified; however, the Department of Labor (DOL) may audit or revoke a certification for cause, and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) will independently assess at the I-140 stage whether the beneficiary was qualified for the offered position             as of the priority date (March 15, 2025)    . The credential gaps identified below are therefore most consequential for (i) PERM audit/revocation exposure and (ii) the forthcoming I-140 petition. The concurrently planned H-1B (I-129) petition is a separate filing governed by a distinct         “    specialty occupation    ”         standard and is not the subject of this analysis, although several of the same documents will be reused.  
      
        II. Documents Reviewed  
              ETA Form 9089         — PERM application, Case No. A-18247-63910 (incl. addenda to §§ H.10, J.B.5, J.C).  
              Dr. Mehrotra’s résumé/CV.  
              Experience letter — DataBridge Solutions LLC    , signed by Dr. Samuel Okonkwo, VP of Data Science, dated April 10, 2025.  
              Experience letter — Evalpoint Technologies Pvt. Ltd.    , signed by Vikram Reddy, Director of Engineering, dated March 28, 2025.  
              NC State University official transcript         — M.S. in Computer Science, conferred May 12, 2018 (transcript issued May 20, 2025).  
              Credential evaluation report — International Academic Evaluators (IAE)    , Report No. IAE-2025-02-4738, dated February 15, 2025 (B.Tech, MIT-Pune).  
              Professional certifications compilation         — Whitmore & Callahan LLP, June 2025.  
              Prevailing Wage Determination (PWD)         — Tracking No. P-200-24187-329156, issued November 8, 2024.  
              HR-to-counsel email         — M. Foss to C. Halloran, June 4, 2025 (transmitting the package and flagging the missing NC State Research Assistant letter).  
      
        III. Summary of the PERM’s Stated Minimum Requirements (Section J.B)  
                         Requirement  
          As stated on the ETA-9089  
                Education (J.B.1)  
          Master’s degree in Computer Science, Machine Learning, or a closely related field.  
                Experience (J.B.3)  
          5 years of progressive post-master’s experience in machine learning engineering or a closely related occupation.  
                Special skills (J.B.5)  
          Experience must include: (a) designing/deploying production-grade deep learning models using TensorFlow or PyTorch; (b) distributed computing frameworks including Apache Spark; (c) NLP pipeline development; (d) cloud-based ML deployment on AWS SageMaker or Google Vertex AI; (e) proficiency in Python             and R    .             AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or equivalent required.         Up to 10% domestic travel. Will supervise a team of 3–5 ML engineers.  
                Attestations (K.1–K.5)  
          K.1 meets education minimum —             Yes    ; K.2 meets experience minimum —             Yes         (~7 yrs post-master’s); K.3 possesses special skills/other requirements —             Yes    ; K.4 qualified for the position —             Yes    ; K.5 gained qualifying experience with petitioning employer —             No    .  
         
        IV. Executive Summary  
      The beneficiary’s education (M.S. in Computer Science, NC State, 2018) and the             quantity         of her post-master’s experience (~6 years 9 months at DataBridge, exceeding the 5-year minimum) are adequately documented. However, the credential file             does not fully support several stated minimum requirements    , and one gap is material:  
              CRITICAL — The beneficiary does not hold the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification         that Section J.B.5 makes a mandatory minimum (    “    or equivalent required    ”    ). She holds only an             expired         AWS Cloud Practitioner (foundational) and a Google TensorFlow Developer Certificate, neither of which is equivalent. The certifications compilation expressly confirms she holds no AWS specialty certification. This is inconsistent with the         “    Yes    ”         attestations at K.3 and K.4 and creates PERM revocation exposure and I-140 denial risk.  
              MEDIUM —                 “        Proficiency in R        ”                 is not corroborated         by the DataBridge experience letter, which characterizes R usage as         “    some use … ad hoc,    ”         undercutting the stated proficiency requirement.  
              MEDIUM — Supervisory scope gap:         the position requires supervising 3–5 ML engineers; the documented experience is leading 2 junior data scientists.  
              MEDIUM — Missing NC State Research Assistant experience letter         for a position listed on the application.  
              MEDIUM — FEIN discrepancy         between the PWD (84-3291047) and the PERM (84-3291756).  
          Several lower-priority documentation/accuracy items (experience-figure overstatement, transcript and experience-letter dating, compilation captioning).  
      A consolidated findings table appears in Section V; detailed analysis and remedial recommendations follow in Sections V and VI.  
      
        V. Detailed Gap Analysis  
       A. Consolidated Findings Table  
                            #  
          PERM Requirement / Item  
          Document of Record  
          Gap Identified  
          Severity  
            1  
          J.B.5 — AWS Certified ML – Specialty cert         “    or equivalent required    ”  
          Certifications compilation; résumé  
          Beneficiary holds             no         AWS specialty cert; only expired AWS Cloud Practitioner + Google TensorFlow Developer. Not equivalent.  
              Critical  
            2  
          K.3 / K.4 — attests beneficiary meets all special requirements and is qualified  
          ETA-9089 §§ K.3, K.4  
          Attestations inconsistent with Finding 1 (cert not held). Potential false attestation.  
              Critical  
            3  
          J.B.5(e) —         “    proficiency in Python and R    ”  
          DataBridge letter; résumé; transcript  
          Letter says         “    some use of R … ad hoc    ”    ; résumé lists R as         “    intermediate.    ”         Undercuts         “    proficiency.    ”  
              Medium  
            4  
          J.B.5 —         “    Will supervise a team of 3–5 ML engineers    ”  
          DataBridge letter; résumé  
          Documented supervision = 2 junior             data scientists         (not 3–5; not         “    ML engineers    ”    ).  
              Medium  
            5  
          J.C, Entry 2 — NC State Research Assistant (Aug 2016–May 2018)  
          Transcript only; no experience letter  
          No verification letter (Prof. Tsai on sabbatical). Transcript shows appointment but not duties.  
              Medium  
            6  
          C.7 — Employer FEIN  
          PERM C.7 vs. PWD §1  
          PERM FEIN 84-3291756 ≠ PWD FEIN 84-3291047.  
              Medium  
            7  
          K.2 —         “    approximately 7 years    ”         post-master’s  
          ETA-9089 § K.2  
          Actual ≈ 6 yrs 9 mos at filing. Overstated (still > 5-yr minimum).  
          Low  
            8  
          Supporting evidence dating  
          NC State transcript  
          Transcript issued May 20, 2025 — after the March 15, 2025 filing.  
          Low  
            9  
          Document captioning  
          Certifications compilation  
          Captioned         “    Compiled for H-1B Petition Filing    ”    ; references         “    H-1B petition.    ”  
          Low  
            10  
          Experience-letter dating  
          DataBridge (4/10/25); Evalpoint (3/28/25)  
          Both post-date filing. Acceptable for audit (experience predates filing).  
          Informational  
            11  
          J.C, Entry 1 — Evalpoint experience  
          Evalpoint letter  
          ML exposure         “    ancillary and secondary    ”    ; does not support special skills (pre-master’s).  
          Informational  
           B. Critical Findings  
       Finding 1 — Required AWS certification not held (J.B.5)  
      Section J.B.5 states that the         “    AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or equivalent [is] required,    ”         and the addendum to J.B.5 reiterates this as a mandatory minimum. The certifications compilation documents only two credentials:  
              AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner         — foundational level, issued January 18, 2022,             expired January 18, 2025         (i.e., expired             before         the March 15, 2025 filing and not renewed); and  
              TensorFlow Developer Certificate         — issued March 10, 2023 by             Google    , no expiration, framework-specific to TensorFlow.  
      The compilation expressly states:             “        Counsel specifically inquired whether the beneficiary holds the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or any other AWS specialty-level certification; the beneficiary confirmed she does not hold any such certification.        ”  
          Analysis.         Neither held credential is an         “    equivalent    ”         to the AWS ML – Specialty certification:  
          The AWS Cloud Practitioner is a             foundational         credential covering general cloud literacy; it does not test machine-learning specialization, SageMaker, or model deployment, and it is             expired    . At the priority date the beneficiary therefore held             no valid AWS certification of any kind    .  
          The TensorFlow Developer Certificate is issued by             Google    , is             framework-specific         (TensorFlow model building), and does not cover AWS ML services, deployment, or ML operations that the AWS ML – Specialty tests and that the position itself requires (AWS SageMaker deployment per J.B.5(d)). It is not a functional substitute.  
      Because the certification is framed as a mandatory minimum (    “    required    ”    ), the beneficiary does not meet a stated minimum special requirement as of the priority date.  
          Risk.         (i)             PERM revocation         — DOL may revoke a certification within one year of filing for         “    cause    ”         including misrepresentation or a material error in the application (20 CFR 656.32). (ii)             I-140 denial         — USCIS verifies that the beneficiary possessed the minimum requirements (including special requirements) as of the priority date; a missing mandatory certification is a common basis for denial. (iii)             Business-necessity/consistency exposure         — if the certification is not in fact a pre-hire minimum, its inclusion as a         “    required    ”         minimum is itself problematic (it must be job-related, consistent with business necessity under 20 CFR 656.17(h), and applied uniformly to U.S. workers).  
        Finding 2 — Attestation inconsistency (K.3 / K.4)  
      Sections K.3 and K.4 attest         “    Yes    ”         that the beneficiary         “    possess[es] the special skills and other requirements stated in Section J.B.5    ”         and         “    is qualified for the job opportunity.    ”         Because J.B.5 includes the AWS ML – Specialty certification as a required minimum, and the beneficiary does not hold it (Finding 1), these attestations are             not supported by the credential file         and are potentially inaccurate. This is the legal consequence of Finding 1 and should be treated as a single, integrated issue for remediation.  
         C. Medium Findings  
       Finding 3 —         “    Proficiency in R    ”         not corroborated (J.B.5(e))  
      J.B.5(e) requires         “    proficiency in Python and R programming languages.    ”         The DataBridge experience letter — the principal document supporting the qualifying experience — states that the beneficiary         “    Used Python as the primary programming language,             with some use of R for statistical reporting and ad hoc data analysis    .    ”         The résumé lists R as         “        intermediate    ”         (versus Python         “    expert    ”    ). The NC State transcript shows one relevant course,             ST 558 — Statistical Computing with R (grade A-)    , which provides academic support but not work-experience corroboration.  
          Analysis.         The experience letter’s own language (    “    some use,    ”         “    ad hoc    ”    ) is             below         the         “    proficiency    ”         standard stated in J.B.5(e) and, if produced in an audit, could be read to             contradict         the requirement rather than satisfy it. Python proficiency is well supported; R proficiency is the weak point.  
        Finding 4 — Supervisory scope gap (J.B.5)  
      J.B.5 states the position         “    Will supervise a team of 3–5 ML engineers.    ”         The DataBridge letter and the résumé both document that the beneficiary         “        Led a team of 2 junior data scientists    .    ”  
          Analysis.         Two issues: (i)             quantitative         — 2 reports is below the 3–5 range stated; and (ii)             terminological         — the reports were         “    junior data scientists,    ”         not         “    ML engineers.    ”         If prior supervisory experience of a 3–5-person team is a true pre-hire minimum, the documented experience falls short. If supervision of 3–5 is a forward-looking job duty (the work to be performed at Prismex) rather than a pre-hire minimum, the beneficiary’s demonstrated supervisory capability (leading 2) may suffice, but the application should not be read to require prior 3–5-person supervisory experience that the file does not document.  
        Finding 5 — Missing NC State Research Assistant experience letter (J.C, Entry 2)  
      The application lists the NC State Research Assistant position (August 15, 2016 – May 15, 2018, 20 hrs/week) as Experience Entry 2 (    “    related occupation    ”    ). The HR email confirms             no experience verification letter         has been obtained (Professor Helen Tsai is on sabbatical and unresponsive). The transcript documents the appointment, supervisor, and NSF funding source (Grant No. IIS-1742956) but does             not         describe duties.  
          Analysis.         Because this experience was gained             during         the master’s program and is             part-time    , it is             not counted         toward the 5-year post-master’s minimum (K.2 correctly relies only on DataBridge). The impact is therefore limited. Nonetheless, it is a listed experience entry, and in an audit DOL expects each claimed experience entry to be documented; the transcript alone is a partial substitute.  
        Finding 6 — FEIN discrepancy (C.7 vs. PWD §1)  
      The PERM application (C.7) lists the employer FEIN as             84-3291756    . The Prevailing Wage Determination (§1) lists the employer FEIN as             84-3291047    . The employer name and address match across both documents, but the FEINs differ.  
          Analysis.         The FEIN is a key employer identifier that must be consistent across the PWD, the PERM, and the I-140. A mismatch suggests a clerical error on one document and must be reconciled before the I-140 is filed. If the PWD bears an incorrect FEIN, counsel should assess any impact on the validity of the prevailing-wage support for the certified PERM.  
         D. Lower-Priority / Informational Findings  
              Finding 7 —                 “        Approximately 7 years        ”                 overstatement (K.2).         From June 4, 2018 to the March 15, 2025 priority date is approximately             6 years 9 months    , not 7 years. The figure still exceeds the 5-year minimum, but it should be stated accurately in any audit response or I-140 support.  
              Finding 8 — Transcript issue date post-dates filing.         The NC State transcript is dated May 20, 2025 (after the March 15, 2025 filing and just before the May 22, 2025 certification). The degree was conferred May 12, 2018, so the beneficiary was qualified at filing; however, the transcript copy in the record post-dates the priority date. Retain degree-conferral evidence dated on or before filing for audit readiness.  
              Finding 9 — Certifications compilation mis-captioned.         The compilation is captioned         “    Compiled for             H-1B Petition Filing    ”         and references         “    the above-referenced             H-1B petition    ,    ”         yet it is being used to support the PERM. It was also prepared by partner Jonathan R. Whitmore rather than the attorney of record (Craig Halloran) — same firm. Re-issue with a PERM-appropriate caption for consistency.  
              Finding 10 — Experience letters post-date filing (informational).         The DataBridge (April 10, 2025) and Evalpoint (March 28, 2025) letters post-date the filing. This is acceptable for PERM audit purposes because the underlying experience predates the filing; no action required beyond retention.  
              Finding 11 — Evalpoint experience does not support special skills (informational).         The Evalpoint letter candidly states the beneficiary’s ML exposure there was         “    ancillary and secondary.    ”         This pre-master’s software-engineering experience is not counted toward the 5-year post-master’s minimum and is not relied upon for the special skills (which rest on the DataBridge experience). No action required; confirm the audit file does not inadvertently rely on Evalpoint for special-skills qualification.  
        E. Additional Observation (non-credential)  
      The job title         “    Senior Machine Learning Engineer    ”         is classified under             SOC 15-2051 (Data Scientists)         consistently across both the PWD and the PERM, and the PWD’s stated minimums (Master’s degree; 5 years) match the PERM. While         “    Machine Learning Engineer    ”         might also be argued to map to other computer-occupation SOCs, the PWD and PERM are internally consistent, so this is             not         flagged as a gap. Counsel should simply confirm the SOC selection remains appropriate at the I-140 stage.  
      
         VI. Remedial Recommendations  
       Priority 1 — Cure the certification gap (Findings 1 & 2)  
              Obtain the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification immediately.         Schedule the beneficiary for the exam at the earliest available date. This is the most direct cure and should be completed             before         the I-140 is filed and before any audit response is required.  
              Reconcile the nature of the requirement.         Determine, on the record, whether the AWS ML – Specialty certification is (a) a             true pre-hire minimum         that the beneficiary must have held as of the priority date, or (b) a             hire/post-offer condition         to be satisfied before commencement. This determination drives the remediation path:  
              If a true pre-filing minimum:         the beneficiary did not possess it at the priority date, creating I-140 denial risk and PERM revocation exposure. Evaluate whether to (i) proceed to the I-140 with the certification now obtained, supported by a business-necessity justification and a prepared audit defense, or (ii) withdraw and             re-file a new PERM         with the certification obtained             before         the new filing and with consistent K.3/K.4 attestations. The latter is the cleaner but slower path and must be weighed against the beneficiary’s STEM-OPT expiration (September 15, 2025).  
              If a hire/post-offer condition:         it should not have been stated as a         “    required    ”         minimum in J.B.5, and the K.3/K.4 attestations are overstated. Document the employer’s actual hiring practice (i.e., that the certification is required before start, not before filing) and assess whether the discrepancy is curable by explanation or whether a new filing is warranted to avoid an inconsistency the DOL could challenge.  
              Renew or replace the expired AWS Cloud Practitioner    , or rely on the new specialty certification as the operative AWS credential. Do not present the expired Cloud Practitioner as a current qualification.  
              Build an audit-defense file         documenting (i) the business necessity of the certification requirement under 20 CFR 656.17(h) (job-relatedness to a Senior ML Engineer role deploying on AWS SageMaker), and (ii) the uniform application of the requirement to all applicants during recruitment.  
        Priority 2 — Corroborate R proficiency (Finding 3)  
              Obtain a revised or supplemental DataBridge experience letter         that accurately and specifically describes the beneficiary’s use of R — the types of statistical reporting/analyses performed, frequency, complexity, and independence — to support         “    proficiency.    ”         If R use was genuinely limited,             reassess whether                 “        proficiency in R        ”                 is a true minimum    ; if it is not, consider whether the requirement should be softened (e.g.,         “    working knowledge of R    ”    ) to avoid an inconsistency between the stated minimum and the experience letter. Any change to the stated minimum has PERM implications and must be evaluated carefully given the certification is already issued.  
              Supplement         with the ST 558 transcript entry and, if available, examples of R-based statistical reporting deliverables (redacted as needed).  
        Priority 3 — Address the supervisory scope (Finding 4)  
              Clarify whether prior supervision of a 3–5-person ML-engineer team is a true pre-hire minimum.         If it is a forward-looking job duty, document that the beneficiary’s demonstrated supervisory experience (leading 2 junior data scientists, plus mentorship/code review) satisfies the supervisory             capability         requirement. If prior 3–5-person supervision is a true minimum, obtain a supplemental letter detailing the full scope of her leadership responsibilities, or reassess the requirement as above.  
        Priority 4 — Document the Research Assistant experience (Finding 5)  
              Continue efforts to obtain an NC State experience letter.         Per the HR email, pursue the department administrator or university HR as a backup to Professor Tsai. If a letter cannot be obtained, rely on the transcript (which documents the appointment, supervisor, and NSF funding)             plus         a detailed sworn personal statement from the beneficiary describing her RA duties, corroborated by her two co-authored conference publications (EMNLP 2018; ACL 2017). Because this experience is not counted toward the 5-year post-master’s minimum, the residual risk is limited.  
        Priority 5 — Reconcile the FEIN (Finding 6)  
              Immediately verify the correct FEIN         against the employer’s IRS records/tax filings. Determine which document (PWD or PERM) bears the error. If the PWD is incorrect, assess whether a corrected PWD is needed and the impact, if any, on the certified PERM.             Ensure the I-140 uses the correct, consistent FEIN         and document the reconciliation in the file.  
        Priority 6 — Documentation clean-up (Findings 7–9)  
              State the experience duration accurately         (~6 years 9 months as of the priority date) in any audit response or I-140 support letter; do not repeat         “    approximately 7 years.    ”  
              Obtain/retain degree-conferral evidence dated on or before the priority date         (e.g., a prior transcript copy or the NC State degree-conferral verification) for audit readiness, in addition to the May 20, 2025 transcript.  
              Re-issue the certifications compilation         with a PERM-appropriate caption (and have the attorney of record, Craig Halloran, sign or co-sign) for consistency across the PERM/I-140 record.  
      
         VII. Risk Assessment and Recommended Path  
                           Risk  
          Likelihood  
          Impact  
          Primary Mitigation  
            I-140 denial — beneficiary lacked a stated minimum (cert) as of priority date  
          Medium-High  
          High  
          Obtain AWS ML – Specialty; reconcile requirement; consider new PERM if not curable  
            PERM revocation for cause (20 CFR 656.32) — inaccurate K.3/K.4 attestations  
          Medium  
          High  
          Same as above; prepare audit defense  
            Audit challenge to R proficiency / supervisory scope  
          Medium  
          Medium  
          Revised DataBridge letter; clarify requirements  
            FEIN inconsistency causing I-140 RFE/delay  
          Medium  
          Medium  
          Reconcile FEIN before I-140 filing  
            Documentation gaps (RA letter, dating, captioning)  
          Low-Medium  
          Low-Medium  
          Supplement and clean up as noted  
             Recommended path.         The education and experience-quantity requirements are satisfied. The dispositive issue is the             AWS certification gap    , which must be resolved before the I-140 is filed. We recommend: (1) immediate enrollment of the beneficiary in the AWS ML – Specialty exam; (2) a same-week decision on whether the certification is a pre-filing minimum or a hire condition, which determines whether the existing certified PERM can be salvaged or a new filing is advisable; (3) parallel execution of the Priority 2–6 remediation items; and (4) assembly of a complete audit-defense file. Given the beneficiary’s STEM-OPT expiration on September 15, 2025, these items should be expedited, but the certification question should not be papered over to meet a filing deadline — an I-140 filed on an unsupported minimum is likely to be denied and could draw scrutiny back to the certified PERM.  
      
        VIII. Conclusion  
      The PERM application is internally consistent on education, prevailing wage, and the broad experience quantity, and the beneficiary plainly exceeds the 5-year post-master’s experience threshold. However, the supporting credentials do             not         fully substantiate every stated minimum requirement. The single most serious gap is the             absence of the required AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification    , which is inconsistent with the K.3/K.4 attestations and creates material I-140 and revocation risk. Secondary gaps in R-proficiency corroboration, supervisory scope, the missing RA letter, and the FEIN discrepancy are readily curable with supplemental documentation and reconciliation. We recommend the remediation steps in Section VI be initiated immediately and completed before the I-140 is filed, with a decision on the certification requirement’s character (pre-filing minimum vs. hire condition) made this week.  
      We are available to discuss any of the above and to assist with the revised experience letters, the audit-defense file, and the I-140 strategy.  
          — End of Memorandum —
